title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
2.4 Descriptive Statistics of Engagement Metrics
Table 2 shows the descriptive statistics of the numeric variables. As a supplement, Table 3 shows the count and proportion of zero and non-zero values among these numeric variables.
The descriptive statistics across all three engagement metrics (counts of likes, replies, and retweets) show a highly skewed distribution, indicating that the majority of tweets in this dataset received no engagement. Among the metrics, likes are the most common form of interaction, with a mean of 3.04 and 40.40% of tweets receiving at least one like, followed closely by retweets at a mean of 2.35 and 41.40% non-zero values. Replies were notably rare, with only 7.07% of tweets receiving any response and a mean of just 0.14. The relatively large standard deviations, combined with the substantial gaps between the 75th percentile values (0 or 1) and the maximum values (898 likes, 83 replies, 367 retweets), suggest the presence of a small number of highly viral tweets that disproportionately inflate the averages.


Code
num_vars <- c("likes_count", "replies_count", "retweets_count")

# compute summary statistics for each numeric variable and pivot to long format
num_stats_table <- data |>
  summarise(across(
    all_of(num_vars),
    list(
      mean   = ~round(mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      sd     = ~round(sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      min    = ~min(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
      q1     = ~quantile(.x, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
      median = ~median(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
      q3     = ~quantile(.x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
      max    = ~max(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    .names = "{.col}###{.fn}"
  )) |>
  # "###" is a collision-safe delimiter 
  # for splitting "col###stat" in pivot_longer
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to  = c("Variable", ".value"),
    names_sep = "###"
  )

knitr::kable(
  num_stats_table |>
    rename(
      Mean = mean, SD = sd, Min = min,
      `25%` = q1, Median = median, `75%` = q3, Max = max
    ),
  align = c('l', 'c', 'c', 'c', 'c', 'c', 'c', 'c'),
  caption = "Descriptive Statistics Summary of Numeric Variables"
)

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
2.5 Date Variable
The date variable involved in this study is date_utc, derived from the original created_at variable and normalized to UTC (see Appendix for the conversion process). It ranges from January 27th, 2017, to July 19th, 2021, covering a total time span of 5 years (1,634 days).
The unit of analysis over time in this study is each day. Figure 1 shows the daily distribution of tweets, where the x-axis displays the time range of the dataset (January 27th, 2017, to July 19th, 2021) and the y-axis represents the counts of observed tweets by day. Overall, tweet volume remained relatively low and stable in the earlier years of the dataset, generally fluctuating between 0 and 20 tweets per day, before showing a notable increase in activity around 2020–2021, with several prominent spikes, including one approaching 100 tweets in a single day, suggesting a growing public interest in AI-related discourse toward the end of the observation period.


Code
cat("Date Range (yyyy-mm-dd):\n",
    as.character(range(data$date_utc)[1]), "to",
    as.character(range(data$date_utc)[2]), "\n")
max(data$date_utc) - min(data$date_utc)


Date Range (yyyy-mm-dd):
 2017-01-27 to 2021-07-19 
Time difference of 1634 days




Code
daily_count <- data |>
  count(date_utc)

min_date <- min(daily_count$date_utc)
max_date <- max(daily_count$date_utc)
# include both endpoints explicitly 
# so the axis always shows actual start/end dates
x_breaks <- sort(unique(c(min_date, seq(min_date, max_date, by = "9 months"), max_date)))

ggplot(daily_count, aes(x = date_utc, y = n)) +
  geom_area(fill = "rosybrown", alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_x_date(breaks = x_breaks, date_labels = "%m-%d-%Y") +
  labs(
    title = "Daily Distribution of AI-Related Tweets (2017–2021)",
    x = "Timeline (Daily Unit of Analysis)",
    y = "Number of Tweets"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x  = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10)),
    axis.title.y = element_text(margin = margin(r = 10)),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  )








Figure 1: Daily Distribution of AI-Related Tweets (2017–2021)

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
2.6 Text Variables
There are three textual variables involved in this study: text, hashtags, and cashtags.

2.6.1 Text
The text variable contains the unique textual content of each tweet, with no missing values (NAs) nor empty content (empty string ""), with a sample size of 3,000. The number of unique values in the text variable is 2,995. For example, "Brands Can't Afford to Underestimate the Power of Artificial Intelligence, OMD's Retail Revolution https://t.co/l1hSywqKgM #ai #ml #dl" is one of the values.

2.6.2 Hashtags
The hashtags is another textual variable. It contains lists of lowercase hashtags starting with the # symbol in each tweet (all hashtags from the original tweets were converted to lowercase), with no missing values (NAs). Specifically, it contains 2,059 non-empty values (i.e., 2,059 observations contain one or more hashtags), where there are 1,539 non-empty unique values (excluding "[]"). For instance, "['ai', 'ml', 'dl']" and "['artificialintelligence', 'ai']" are two of the values.
In addition, as each tweet can contain multiple hashtags, the values are separated into single hashtags to gain more nuanced insights (see Appendix for the separation process). As observed, there are 2,382 non-empty unique single hashtags. For example, "ai" and "ml" are two of the unique single hashtags.

Likewise, the cashtags variable contains lists of lowercase cashtags in each tweet, which are clickable financial ticker symbols on Twitter (now X), formed by prefixing a stock or cryptocurrency abbreviation with the $ sign. title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
3.1 Statistical Methods
Prior to model estimation, correlation analysis is conducted as an exploratory step to examine the pairwise relationships between thematic features, sentiment scores, and user engagement metrics. This preliminary analysis helps identify potential associations and informs the selection of variables for subsequent modeling, while also providing an initial understanding of the direction and strength of relationships within the dataset.
In the meantime, this paper employs Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) to generate models to examine the relationship between variables. GLMs were chosen over simple linear regression due to the nature of the engagement data containing a preponderance of zero values, which is a distributional characteristic that standard linear models may not adequately handle. By accommodating non-normal response distributions, GLMs offer a more flexible and appropriate modeling approach for count-based social media engagement data.

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
3.2 Text-as-Data Methods
For text analysis, this paper draws on two complementary computational techniques. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling, which is a widely used unsupervised machine learning technique, is applied to uncover potential thematic structures within the dataset, identifying the core subjects that characterized AI-related discourse. Meanwhile, sentiment analysis with lexicons, a natural language processing approach evaluating the emotional tone of textual data by leveraging predefined lexical resources to classify polarity (e.g., positive, negative, or neutral), is also employed to establish the overall emotional polarity and trends among tweets. In addition, Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) is used to track how the salience of key AI-related terms shifted from 2017 to 2021, offering a longitudinal perspective on lexical change as AI moved toward mainstream adoption.
These methods directly support the research questions investigating how identified themes and sentiments polarities are associated with user engagement, as Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis allow the study to identify underlying themes and sentiments, while TF-IDF provides a temporal perspective on the evolving trends of AI-related key terms across the dataset. Together, these techniques inform the subsequent inferential analysis, in which GLM provide insights into the predictive relationships between textual variables and engagement metrics statistically.

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
4.1 LDA Topic Modeling
To identify the underlying thematic structure of AI-related tweets, LDA was applied to the preprocessed tweet corpus. After tokenization, tweets were filtered using a custom stopword list that removed general English stopwords, Twitter-specific terms (e.g., "rt", "tweet"), and the search keyword "artificial intelligence". A TF-IDF filter then retained only words appearing in more than 0.1% but fewer than 75% of all documents, reducing vocabulary noise while preserving informative terms. The resulting document-term matrix served as input to the LDA model, which was estimated with K = 5 topics using Gibbs sampling with 2,000 iterations and a 500-iteration burn-in period.


Code
# custom domain-specific stopwords
mystopwords <- c(
  stop_words$word,
  "comment", "post",
  "twitter", "tweet", "tweets",
  "rt", "retweet", "retweets",
  "artificial", "intelligence"
)
mystopwords <- tolower(unique(mystopwords))
cat("Total stopwords:", length(mystopwords), "\n")

# tokenize
tm_token_data <- data |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(data.frame(word = mystopwords)) |>
  mutate(nchar = nchar(word)) |>
  filter(nchar > 2) |>
  filter(!grepl("[0-9]{1}", word)) |>
  filter(!grepl("\\W", word))

cat("Total tokens:", nrow(tm_token_data), "\n")


Total stopwords: 738 
Total tokens: 27529 




Code
n_docs_total <- n_distinct(tm_token_data$tweet_index)

# retain words in (0.1%, 75%) document frequency range
good_words <- tm_token_data |>
  count(tweet_index, word) |>
  group_by(word) |>
  summarise(doc_freq = n() / n_docs_total, .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(doc_freq < 0.75, doc_freq > 0.001)

tm_token_data_pruned <- tm_token_data |>
  inner_join(good_words, by = "word")

cat("Vocabulary after TF-IDF filter:", nrow(good_words), "words\n")
cat("Tokens after filter:", nrow(tm_token_data_pruned), "\n")

# document-term matrix
tweet_dtm <- tm_token_data_pruned |>
  count(tweet_index, word) |>
  cast_dtm(tweet_index, word, n)

cat("DTM:", nrow(tweet_dtm), "documents x", ncol(tweet_dtm), "terms\n")


Vocabulary after TF-IDF filter: 1799 words
Tokens after filter: 20094 
DTM: 2961 documents x 1799 terms




Code
set.seed(32)
lda_model <- LDA(
  tweet_dtm, k = 5, method = "Gibbs",
  control = list(seed = 32, iter = 2000, burnin = 500, verbose = 0)
)
# persist to disk so the object survives 
# chunk-cache invalidation and is usable externally
save(lda_model, file = "./lda_model_ai.RData")


Figure 2 displays the top 10 words by probability (beta) for each of the five topics, providing the basis for thematic interpretation. Based on the word distributions, the topics were labeled as follows: Topic 1 represents robotics and automation, with key terms such as robotics, automation, and robot; Topic 2 reflects technology, research, and financial markets, featuring words like blockchain, technology, research, market, and healthcare; Topic 3 captures data science, machine learning, and security, with terms including data, learning, machine, and security; Topic 4 represents business-oriented AI innovation, characterized by words like business, future, innovation, digital, and digitaltransformation; and Topic 5 emphasizes applied AI ecosystem vocabulary, including machinelearning, bigdata, datascience, iot, and deeplearning.


Code
lda_beta <- tidy(lda_model, matrix = "beta")

lda_beta |>
  group_by(topic) |>
  slice_max(beta, n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(term = reorder_within(term, beta, topic)) |>
  ggplot(aes(beta, term, fill = factor(topic))) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~topic, scales = "free") +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Words per LDA Topic (K = 5)",
    x = "Beta (Word Probability)", 
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  )








Figure 2: Top 10 Words per LDA Topic (K = 5) Note. Bars represent word probability (beta) within each topic. Topics are labeled by their dominant semantic cluster.





Table 4 presents the distribution of dominant topics, which is the topic with the highest membership probability for each tweet, across the 3,000 sampled tweets. Topic 1 (robotics and automation) was the most prevalent, accounting for 28.4% of tweets, followed by Topic 2 (technology and markets, 23.3%), Topic 3 (data science and security, 19.0%), Topic 4 (business and innovation, 15.3%), and Topic 5 (AI ecosystem buzzwords, 14.0%). The distribution was moderately uneven, with Topics 1 and 2 together covering more than half of all tweets.


Code
# gamma matrix: document-topic probabilities
lda_gamma <- tidy(lda_model, matrix = "gamma")

# reshape wide and join with original data
gamma_wide <- lda_gamma |>
  mutate(tweet_index = as.integer(document)) |>
  select(-document) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = topic, values_from = gamma, names_prefix = "topic_")

meta_theta_df <- data |>
  inner_join(gamma_wide, by = "tweet_index")




Code
# assign each tweet its dominant topic (highest gamma) and tabulate counts
dominant_dist <- lda_gamma |>
  group_by(document) |>
  slice_max(gamma, n = 1) |>
  ungroup() |>
  count(topic) |>
  mutate(pct = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1))

knitr::kable(
  dominant_dist |>
    rename(
      Topic = topic,
      `Tweet Count` = n,
      `Percentage (%)` = pct
    ),
  align = c('c','c','c'),
  caption = "Distribution of Dominant LDA Topics Across Tweets"
)




Table 4: Distribution of Dominant LDA Topics Across Tweets











Topic
Tweet Count
Percentage (%)




1
865
20.7


2
779
18.7


3
837
20.1


4
833
20.0


5
855
20.5








Figure 3 traces average topic probabilities (gamma) across years from 2017 to 2021. Notably, all five topics maintained average probabilities close to 0.20 throughout the study period, indicating a relatively stable thematic structure over time. Despite this overall stability, subtle directional shifts were present. Topic 4 (business and innovation) showed a modest upward trend from 2017 to 2021, while Topic 2 (technology and markets) declined slightly. Topic 5 (AI ecosystem buzzwords) also edged upward by 2021. These patterns suggest that while the broad thematic balance of AI discourse remained intact, business- and implementation-oriented language gradually gained somewhat greater prominence as AI moved toward mainstream adoption.


Code
# compute mean topic probability (gamma) per year for trend visualization
topics_by_year <- lda_gamma |>
  mutate(tweet_index = as.integer(document)) |>
  left_join(data |> select(tweet_index, date_utc), by = "tweet_index") |>
  mutate(year = year(date_utc)) |>
  group_by(year, topic) |>
  summarise(mean_gamma = mean(gamma), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(topics_by_year, aes(x = year, y = mean_gamma, color = factor(topic))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2017:2021) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "1" = "coral", "2" = "darkgoldenrod3", "3" = "darkseagreen",
      "4" = "steelblue", "5" = "orchid3"
    ),
    labels = paste("Topic", 1:5)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Topic Prevalence Over Time (2017–2021)",
    x = "Year", 
    y = "Average Topic Probability (Gamma)", 
    color = "Topic"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  )








Figure 3: Topic Prevalence Over Time (2017–2021) Note. Lines represent mean gamma (topic membership probability) across all tweets in each year. All five topics remain close to 0.20 throughout the period, indicating thematic stability.

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
4.2 Hashtag and Cashtag Analysis
Hashtag and cashtag analysis complemented the topic model by examining the explicit user-generated labels that framed AI-related tweets. Table 5 summarizes the overall prevalence of hashtags and cashtags. Among the 3,000 sampled tweets, 2,059 (68.6%) contained at least one hashtag, with an average of 3.45 hashtags per tweet. By contrast, only 22 tweets (0.7%) contained any cashtag, with an average of just 0.01 per tweet. This large difference in frequency suggests that financial and market-oriented labeling through cashtags was marginal in this corpus, while hashtag use was both widespread and dense.


Code
# summarize hashtag and cashtag prevalence across the sample
tag_presence_table <- tibble(
  tag_type = c("Hashtag", "Cashtag"),
  tweets_with_tag = c(
    sum(tag_features$has_hashtag), sum(tag_features$has_cashtag)
  ),
  pct_with_tag    = round(tweets_with_tag / nrow(tag_features) * 100, 1),
  avg_tags_per_tweet = c(
    round(mean(tag_features$n_hashtag), 2),
    round(mean(tag_features$n_cashtag), 2)
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  tag_presence_table |>
    rename(
      `Tag Type`              = tag_type,
      `Tweets With Tag`       = tweets_with_tag,
      `Percent of Tweets (%)` = pct_with_tag,
      `Avg. Tags per Tweet`   = avg_tags_per_tweet
    ),
  align = 'c',
  caption = "Prevalence of Hashtags and Cashtags in the Sample"
)

Percent of Tweets (%)

Table 6 and Figure 4 display the 15 most frequently used hashtags across the full 2017–2021 sample. The top three were #artificialintelligence (1,427 uses), #ai (1,094 uses), and #machinelearning (483 uses), followed by #bigdata, #datascience, #deeplearning, #iot, and #ml. This ranking indicates that AI tweets were predominantly framed through technical and industry labels rather than through social, ethical, or political categories.

# count and rank all single hashtags; keep top 15
top_hashtags <- hashtag_tokens |>
  count(hashtag, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 15)

knitr::kable(
  top_hashtags |> rename(Hashtag = hashtag, Frequency = n),
  align = 'c',
  caption = "Most Frequent Hashtags in AI-Related Tweets (2017–2021)"
)

  mutate(hashtag = reorder(hashtag, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = hashtag)) +

Table 7 presents the top five hashtags by year. #artificialintelligence and #ai remained the top two in every year from 2017 to 2021. However, the third through fifth positions shifted over time: in 2017 and 2018, #bigdata and #iot appeared prominently, while by 2020 and 2021, #datascience and #deeplearning replaced them. The count of #machinelearning grew steadily, from 47 uses in 2017 to 127 in 2021. title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
4.3 Sentiment Analysis

4.3.1 Comparison of Sentiment Lexicons
Sentiment analysis was conducted using three established dictionary lexicons: AFINN, Bing, and NRC. Table 9 summarizes their coverage in the dataset. NRC achieved the broadest coverage at 90.0% of tweets scored, followed by Bing at 78.6%, and AFINN at 42.5%. Figure 6 displays these differences visually. Despite its lower coverage, AFINN was selected as the primary sentiment measure for three reasons. First, AFINN provides a continuous intensity scale (–5 to +5) that captures the degree of positive or negative sentiment, making it directly suitable for regression modeling. Second, among the 1,130 tweets scored by all three lexicons, AFINN correlated strongly with Bing (r = .664) and moderately with NRC (r = .510), while Bing and NRC correlated at r = .528, confirming that all three measures captured a consistent underlying signal. Third, the additional tweets scored by NRC did not justify the increased interpretive complexity of a categorical emotion framework for the purposes of this analysis.


Code
# tokenize and remove stopwords for sentiment analysis
sa_token_data <- data |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  mutate(nchar_w = nchar(word))

cat("Total tokens:", nrow(sa_token_data), "\n")
cat("Unique tokens:", n_distinct(sa_token_data$word), "\n")
cat("Avg tokens per tweet:",
    round(nrow(sa_token_data) / n_distinct(sa_token_data$tweet_index), 1), "\n")


Total tokens: 34090 
Unique tokens: 8259 
Avg tokens per tweet: 11.4 




Code
# AFINN: numeric scores from -5 (very negative) to +5 (very positive)
afinn_scores <- sa_token_data |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = "word") |>
  group_by(tweet_index) |>
  summarise(
    sentiment_afinn = sum(value),
    n_afinn_words   = n(),
    avg_afinn       = mean(value),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

afinn_coverage     <- nrow(afinn_scores)
afinn_coverage_pct <- round(afinn_coverage / nrow(data) * 100, 1)
cat("AFINN coverage:", afinn_coverage, "tweets (", afinn_coverage_pct, "% )\n")


AFINN coverage: 1275 tweets ( 42.5 % )




Code
# Bing: binary positive / negative classification
bing_scores <- sa_token_data |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word") |>
  group_by(tweet_index) |>
  summarise(
    n_positive     = sum(sentiment == "positive"),
    n_negative     = sum(sentiment == "negative"),
    n_bing_words   = n(),
    sentiment_bing = n_positive - n_negative,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

bing_coverage     <- nrow(bing_scores)
bing_coverage_pct <- round(bing_coverage / nrow(data) * 100, 1)
cat("Bing coverage:", bing_coverage, "tweets (", bing_coverage_pct, "%)\n")


Bing coverage: 2359 tweets ( 78.6 %)




Code
# NRC: 8 basic emotions + positive / negative
nrc_raw <- sa_token_data |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("nrc"), by = "word")

nrc_scores <- nrc_raw |>
  group_by(tweet_index, sentiment) |>
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sentiment, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  mutate(sentiment_nrc = positive - negative)

nrc_coverage     <- nrow(nrc_scores)
nrc_coverage_pct <- round(nrc_coverage / nrow(data) * 100, 1)
cat("NRC coverage:", nrc_coverage, "tweets (", nrc_coverage_pct, "%)\n")


NRC coverage: 2700 tweets ( 90 %)




Code
# assemble pre-computed coverage counts into a comparison table
coverage_df <- data.frame(
  lexicon       = c("AFINN", "Bing", "NRC"),
  tweets_scored = c(afinn_coverage, bing_coverage, nrc_coverage),
  coverage_pct  = c(afinn_coverage_pct, bing_coverage_pct, nrc_coverage_pct)
)

knitr::kable(
  coverage_df |>
    rename(
      Lexicon         = lexicon,
      `Tweets Scored` = tweets_scored,
      `Coverage (%)`  = coverage_pct
    ),
  align = 'c',
  caption = "Coverage of Sentiment Lexicons Across the Tweet Sample"
)




Table 9: Coverage of Sentiment Lexicons Across the Tweet Sample






Lexicon
Tweets Scored
Coverage (%)




AFINN
1275
42.5


Bing
2359
78.6


NRC
2700
90.0










Code
# bar chart comparing tweet coverage (%) across the three lexicons
ggplot(coverage_df, aes(x = lexicon, y = coverage_pct, fill = lexicon)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(coverage_pct, "%")), vjust = -0.4, size = 4) +
  ylim(0, 100) +
  labs(
    title = "Sentiment Lexicon Coverage Comparison",
    x = NULL, 
    y = "Coverage (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  )








Figure 6: Sentiment Lexicon Coverage Comparison Note. Bars represent the percentage of the 3,000 tweets that received a sentiment score from each lexicon. NRC achieves the widest coverage; AFINN covers a smaller share but provides continuous intensity scores.







Code
# correlations among tweets scored by all three lexicons
sentiment_cmp <- data |>
  select(tweet_index) |>
  left_join(afinn_scores |> select(tweet_index, sentiment_afinn), by = "tweet_index") |>
  left_join(bing_scores  |> select(tweet_index, sentiment_bing),  by = "tweet_index") |>
  left_join(nrc_scores   |> select(tweet_index, sentiment_nrc),   by = "tweet_index")

complete_cmp <- sentiment_cmp |>
  filter(!is.na(sentiment_afinn), !is.na(sentiment_bing), !is.na(sentiment_nrc))

cat("Tweets scored by all three lexicons:", nrow(complete_cmp), "\n")
cat("AFINN vs Bing: r =",
    round(cor(complete_cmp$sentiment_afinn, complete_cmp$sentiment_bing), 3), "\n")
cat("AFINN vs NRC:  r =",
    round(cor(complete_cmp$sentiment_afinn, complete_cmp$sentiment_nrc), 3), "\n")
cat("Bing  vs NRC:  r =",
    round(cor(complete_cmp$sentiment_bing,  complete_cmp$sentiment_nrc), 3), "\n")


Tweets scored by all three lexicons: 1130 
AFINN vs Bing: r = 0.665 
AFINN vs NRC:  r = 0.509 
Bing  vs NRC:  r = 0.529 




4.3.2 Temporal Trends in Sentiment
Among the 1,275 tweets that received AFINN scores, overall sentiment in the corpus was slightly positive (M = 0.81, Median = 1, range: -18 to 12). Table 10 and Figure 7 present mean AFINN scores by year with 95% confidence intervals. A clear upward trend is visible: mean sentiment increased from 0.33 in 2017 to 0.60 in 2018, 0.80 in 2019, 1.05 in 2020, and 1.11 in 2021. The 95% confidence intervals indicate that this upward movement became increasingly stable in later years. This pattern is consistent with the growing normalization of AI as a practical and promising technology, where public discourse shifted toward greater optimism about AI’s applications and societal benefits.


Code
# attach AFINN scores to data and propagate to meta_theta_df
data <- data |>
  left_join(afinn_scores |> select(tweet_index, sentiment_afinn), by = "tweet_index") |>
  mutate(sentiment_norm = sentiment_afinn / nwords)

meta_theta_df <- meta_theta_df |>
  left_join(afinn_scores |> select(tweet_index, sentiment_afinn), by = "tweet_index")

n_scored <- sum(!is.na(data$sentiment_afinn))
cat("Tweets with AFINN scores:", n_scored,
    paste0("(", round(n_scored / nrow(data) * 100, 1), "%)"), "\n")
summary(data$sentiment_afinn)


Tweets with AFINN scores: 1275 (42.5%) 
    Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max.     NA's 
-18.0000  -1.0000   1.0000   0.8071   2.0000  12.0000     1725 




Code
# yearly mean AFINN scores with standard errors and 95% CIs
sentiment_by_year <- data |>
  filter(!is.na(sentiment_afinn)) |>
  mutate(year = year(date_utc)) |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    mean_sent = mean(sentiment_afinn),
    se_sent   = sd(sentiment_afinn) / sqrt(n),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    ci_low  = mean_sent - 1.96 * se_sent,
    ci_high = mean_sent + 1.96 * se_sent
  )

knitr::kable(
  sentiment_by_year |>
    rename(
      Year = year, `Tweets Scored` = n, `Mean Sentiment` = mean_sent,
      SE = se_sent, `95% CI Low` = ci_low, `95% CI High` = ci_high
    ),
  align = 'c',
  caption = "Mean AFINN Sentiment Score by Year with 95% Confidence Intervals",
  digits  = 3
)

Code
# line + confidence ribbon showing mean AFINN sentiment trend from 2017 to 2021
ggplot(sentiment_by_year, aes(x = year, y = mean_sent)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high), alpha = 0.2, fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", color = "red") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2017:2021) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean AFINN Sentiment by Year (2017–2021)",
    x = "Year",

Figure 7: Mean AFINN Sentiment by Year (2017–2021) Note. Points and line show yearly means; the ribbon represents the 95% confidence interval. The dashed horizontal line marks a score of zero (neutral). Sentiment increased consistently from 2017 to 2021.







4.3.3 Topic–Sentiment Relationship
To examine whether discourse topics were associated with different emotional tones, average AFINN sentiment was computed for each topic using gamma-weighted scores, where each tweet’s contribution to a topic’s average sentiment was proportional to its membership probability in that topic. Figure 8 presents these weighted averages. Sentiment scores were very similar across all five topics, ranging from 0.79 (Topic 5, AI ecosystem buzzwords) to 0.84 (Topic 4, business and innovation). A weighted linear regression model predicting sentiment from topic membership found no statistically significant differences relative to Topic 1 for any other topic (all p > .80). This result shows that in this corpus, emotional tone did not vary meaningfully across thematic clusters; rather, sentiment varied more consistently over time than across topics.


Code
# compute weighted sentiment per topic
meta_theta_df <- meta_theta_df |>
  filter(!is.na(sentiment_afinn)) |>
  mutate(
    wt_sent_t1 = sentiment_afinn * topic_1,
    wt_sent_t2 = sentiment_afinn * topic_2,
    wt_sent_t3 = sentiment_afinn * topic_3,
    wt_sent_t4 = sentiment_afinn * topic_4,
    wt_sent_t5 = sentiment_afinn * topic_5
  )

topic_sent_avg <- tibble(
  topic = 1:5,
  total_weighted = c(
    sum(meta_theta_df$wt_sent_t1), sum(meta_theta_df$wt_sent_t2),
    sum(meta_theta_df$wt_sent_t3), sum(meta_theta_df$wt_sent_t4),
    sum(meta_theta_df$wt_sent_t5)
  ),
  gamma_mass = c(
    sum(meta_theta_df$topic_1), sum(meta_theta_df$topic_2),
    sum(meta_theta_df$topic_3), sum(meta_theta_df$topic_4),
    sum(meta_theta_df$topic_5)
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    avg_sentiment = total_weighted / gamma_mass,
    topic_label   = paste("Topic", topic)
  )




Code
# horizontal bar chart of gamma-weighted average AFINN sentiment per topic
ggplot(
  topic_sent_avg,
  aes(x = reorder(topic_label, avg_sentiment), y = avg_sentiment, fill = topic_label)
) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Weighted Average AFINN Sentiment by Topic",
    x = "Topic", 
    y = "Average AFINN Sentiment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none", 
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  )








Figure 8: Weighted Average AFINN Sentiment by Topic Note. Bars represent the average sentiment score for each topic, weighted by gamma (topic membership probability). The dashed line marks neutral sentiment. Differences across topics are minimal and statistically non-significant.







Code
# weighted regression: sentiment ~ topic (verifies non-significant differences)
sentiment_topic_long <- meta_theta_df |>
  select(tweet_index, sentiment_afinn, starts_with("topic_")) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols         = starts_with("topic_"),
    names_to     = "topic",
    values_to    = "gamma",
    names_prefix = "topic_"
  ) |>
  mutate(topic = factor(topic, levels = as.character(1:5)))

weighted_model <- lm(sentiment_afinn ~ topic, data = sentiment_topic_long, weights = gamma)
summary(weighted_model)

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
4.4 Predictors of User Engagement

4.4.1 Hashtag and Cashtag Features
To examine whether content packaging was associated with engagement (RQ2), mean engagement metrics were first compared by tag presence (Table 11), and then three negative binomial GLMs were fitted predicting likes, replies, and retweets from hashtag presence, hashtag count, cashtag presence, and year (Table 12, Figure 9).
Descriptively, tweets containing hashtags received slightly higher mean likes (3.11 vs. 2.89) and notably higher mean retweets (2.86 vs. 1.23), though slightly fewer replies on average (0.12 vs. 0.19). Tweets with cashtags had higher mean values on all three metrics, but the number of cashtag-containing tweets was too small (n = 22) for reliable comparison.


Code
# compute mean engagement metrics by hashtag and cashtag presence
tag_engagement_summary <- tag_features |>
  summarise(
    likes_no_hashtag    = round(mean(likes_count[!has_hashtag]), 2),
    likes_hashtag       = round(mean(likes_count[has_hashtag]), 2),
    retweets_no_hashtag = round(mean(retweets_count[!has_hashtag]), 2),
    retweets_hashtag    = round(mean(retweets_count[has_hashtag]), 2),
    replies_no_hashtag  = round(mean(replies_count[!has_hashtag]), 2),
    replies_hashtag     = round(mean(replies_count[has_hashtag]), 2),
    likes_no_cashtag    = round(mean(likes_count[!has_cashtag]), 2),
    likes_cashtag       = round(mean(likes_count[has_cashtag]), 2),
    retweets_no_cashtag = round(mean(retweets_count[!has_cashtag]), 2),
    retweets_cashtag    = round(mean(retweets_count[has_cashtag]), 2),
    replies_no_cashtag  = round(mean(replies_count[!has_cashtag]), 2),
    replies_cashtag     = round(mean(replies_count[has_cashtag]), 2)
  )

Table 12 and Figure 9 present the exponentiated regression coefficients (incident rate ratios, IRRs) from the tag-based negative binomial models. The number of hashtags per tweet was a positive predictor of all three engagement outcomes: likes (IRR = 1.08, p < .001), replies (IRR = 1.06, p = .021), and retweets (IRR = 1.13, p < .001). By contrast, simply having any hashtag at all was a negative predictor for likes (IRR = 0.60, p < .001) and replies (IRR = 0.39, p < .001) once hashtag count was included in the model, and was not significant for retweets. This pattern reflects a compositional effect: tweets with only one or two hashtags do not benefit from the discoverability that rich hashtag networks provide, whereas tweets with many hashtags tend to attract more engagement across all metrics. Cashtag presence was not significantly associated with any engagement outcome.


Code
# fit negative binomial GLMs for each engagement metric using tag features
tag_glm_likes <- glm.nb(
  likes_count ~ has_hashtag + has_cashtag + n_hashtag + year,
  data = tag_features
)
tag_glm_replies <- glm.nb(
  replies_count ~ has_hashtag + has_cashtag + n_hashtag + year,
  data = tag_features
)
tag_glm_retweets <- glm.nb(
  retweets_count ~ has_hashtag + has_cashtag + n_hashtag + year,
  data = tag_features
)

tag_models <- list(Likes = tag_glm_likes, Replies = tag_glm_replies, Retweets = tag_glm_retweets)

tag_coef_all <- bind_rows(lapply(names(tag_models), function(nm) {
  tidy(tag_models[[nm]], exponentiate = TRUE) |>
    filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
    mutate(
      # Wald CIs on the log-rate scale (delta method)
      # faster than profile-likelihood confint()
      conf.low  = estimate * exp(-1.96 * std.error),
      conf.high = estimate * exp( 1.96 * std.error),
      outcome   = nm
    )
}))




Code
# extract and format exponentiated coefficients (IRRs) from the tag GLMs
knitr::kable(
  tag_coef_all |>
    select(outcome, term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
    mutate(
      term = recode(term,
        "has_hashtagTRUE" = "Has Hashtag",
        "has_cashtagTRUE" = "Has Cashtag",
        "n_hashtag"       = "Number of Hashtags",
        "year"            = "Year"
      ),
      outcome = factor(outcome, levels = c("Likes", "Replies", "Retweets"))
    ) |>
    rename(
      Outcome = outcome, Predictor = term, IRR = estimate,
      `95% CI Low` = conf.low, `95% CI High` = conf.high, `p` = p.value
    ),
  caption = "Negative Binomial GLMs: Tag Features Predicting User Engagement",
  align = c('l', 'l', 'c', 'c', 'c', 'c'),
  digits  = 3
)

Figure 9: Tag-Based Predictors of Tweet Engagement (IRR Plot) Note. Points represent incident rate ratios (IRRs) and horizontal bars represent 95% confidence intervals. The dashed vertical line at IRR = 1 indicates no effect. IRR > 1 indicates a positive association with engagement.







4.4.2 Sentiment and Topic as Predictors
To directly test the associations between discourse characteristics and engagement, three negative binomial GLMs were fitted predicting likes, replies, and retweets from AFINN sentiment scores, LDA topic probabilities (Topics 1–4, with Topic 5 as the implicit baseline through the compositional constraint), and year fixed effects (Table 13, Figure 10).
AFINN sentiment was not a statistically significant predictor of any engagement outcome. Its IRR was 0.99 for likes (p = .676), 0.92 for replies (p = .081), and 0.98 for retweets (p = .383). This finding indicates that a more positive or more negative emotional tone alone did not reliably increase or decrease engagement once topic composition and year were taken into account.
By contrast, year was a consistent and substantial predictor of engagement. Relative to 2017, tweets from 2018 showed dramatically higher expected likes (IRR = 4.00) and replies (IRR = 8.23), and tweets from 2020 and 2021 also showed significantly higher likes and replies. Retweets were significantly higher in 2018 (IRR = 2.13) and 2021 (IRR = 2.12) relative to 2017. These large year effects suggest that the overall audience for AI content on Twitter grew substantially after 2017, leading to more engagement across the board regardless of the content’s tone.
Topic effects were present for some outcomes but should be interpreted carefully because LDA topic probabilities are compositional—increasing one topic’s probability necessarily reduces the others. Topic 2 showed a strong negative association with likes and retweets, while Topic 4 showed a strong positive association with replies. However, some estimated IRRs were extremely large or small, indicating instability likely due to near-collinearity among topic predictors. The most defensible interpretation is that business-innovation framing (Topic 4) may be associated with more conversational engagement, while certain technology-and-market content (Topic 2) may attract less passive amplification, but these conclusions require further testing with more robust specifications.


Code
# convert year to a factor so GLMs treat it as categorical fixed effects
glm_data <- meta_theta_df |>
  mutate(year = factor(year(date_utc)))

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
5.1 Discussion
Taken together, the results provide partial support for both research questions while revealing important nuances in how AI discourse evolved and how it connected to audience engagement on Twitter between 2017 and 2021.

5.1.1 Thematic Stability with Gradual Specialization (RQ1)
For RQ1, the LDA topic modeling results indicate that AI discourse on Twitter did not undergo a dramatic thematic transformation during the study period. Instead, the corpus showed a relatively stable thematic structure built around five recurring clusters: robotics and automation, technology and markets, data science and security, business and innovation, and applied AI ecosystem vocabulary. The near-constant average topic probabilities across all years (all close to 0.20) suggest a diverse but consistently balanced conversation that absorbed new developments without being reorganized by them.
This finding may appear surprising given that 2017 to 2021 saw substantial milestones in AI, including major advances in natural language processing, growing public debate about AI ethics, and the commercialization of generative AI tools. One explanation is that the keyword-based sampling strategy (“Artificial Intelligence”) may have overrepresented promotional, institutional, and hashtag-driven content, which tends to revolve around a stable set of industry frames regardless of specific technological developments. A corpus built around event-specific terms or date-anchored keywords might reveal sharper thematic shifts.
Despite the overall thematic stability, the hashtag analysis provided a more nuanced story about discursive evolution. While #artificialintelligence and #ai dominated in every year, the lower-ranked hashtags shifted gradually toward more specialized terms such as #machinelearning, #datascience, and #deeplearning, while broader infrastructure terms like #bigdata and #iot declined in relative prominence. This pattern suggests that AI discourse on Twitter evolved less through wholesale topical replacement and more through increasing specificity, and users began categorizing their AI discussions into more precise technical subfields rather than relying solely on the broad “artificial intelligence” framing.
The sentiment trend reinforced and sharpened this picture. The steady increase in mean AFINN sentiment from 0.33 in 2017 to 1.11 in 2021 indicates that public AI discourse became progressively more positive over the five years. This is consistent with the broader narrative of AI mainstreaming, in which the technology became increasingly framed as a source of opportunity, economic value, and practical innovation rather than as an abstract, uncertain, or threatening technological force. Critically, sentiment did not differ meaningfully across topics, where the slight gap between the most positive (Topic 4: 0.84) and least positive (Topic 5: 0.79) topics was not statistically significant. This indicates that optimism about AI was a property of the time period in which a tweet was posted, not of the specific thematic cluster it belonged to.


5.1.2 Engagement Patterns and the Role of Context (RQ2)
For RQ2, the most consistent finding was that user engagement was shaped more strongly by historical context than by the specific tone or topic of individual tweets. Year was a robust predictor across all three engagement models: tweets from 2018 showed dramatically higher expected likes (IRR = 4.00) and replies (IRR = 8.23) compared to those from 2017, and elevated engagement persisted through 2020 and 2021 for likes and replies, and through 2021 for retweets. This pattern suggests that as AI became a more mainstream cultural and economic topic in subsequent years, the Twitter audience for AI content grew larger and more active.
Sentiment, by contrast, was not a significant predictor of engagement once year and topic composition were included in the model. The IRRs for AFINN sentiment were close to 1.00 across all three outcomes and did not reach statistical significance. For content creators and communications practitioners, this is an important finding: crafting more enthusiastic or more alarmist AI content did not appear to translate automatically into greater audience engagement, at least within the context of this corpus. Instead, engagement was tied more to the broader social and platform context in which content was posted.
The hashtag analysis added a more actionable finding to the engagement story. The number of hashtags per tweet was consistently and positively associated with all three engagement metrics in the regression models, while simple hashtag presence (just having at least one hashtag) was negatively associated with likes and replies once hashtag count was controlled. This compositional pattern suggests that richly tagged tweets tend to situate content within multiple streams of existing attention, making them more discoverable and more likely to be engaged with. In contrast, a minimal or token use of hashtags does not reliably confer these discoverability benefits. This is a more specific finding than a simple “use hashtags” recommendation and suggests that the density of hashtagging matters.
Topic effects on engagement were present in some models but were difficult to interpret cleanly due to the compositional nature of LDA topic probabilities, which creates near-multicollinearity among topic predictors and produces inflated or unstable coefficient estimates. The tentative pattern — with business-innovation framing (Topic 4) associated with more conversational replies, and technology-market framing (Topic 2) associated with less passive amplification — is theoretically plausible. Business- and future-oriented AI language may invite debate and personal response, while more abstract technology-market language may be shared and amplified less. However, these conclusions require more stable model specifications before they can be stated with confidence.
Cashtags were too rare (only 22 tweets) to support any strong engagement claims. Their near-absence confirms that financial-sector AI discussion was peripheral in this corpus, even if a small number of tweets connected AI talk to cryptocurrency or stock market speculation.

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
5.2 Limitations
Several limitations qualify these conclusions. First, the analysis used a stratified sample of 3,000 tweets from a corpus of over 893,000 observations, so subtle yearly thematic changes may have been dampened by reduced statistical power. Second, the data were collected using the keyword “Artificial Intelligence,” which may overrepresent institutional, promotional, and professionally framed content while underrepresenting informal, critical, or personal AI discussions. Third, lexicon-based sentiment analysis cannot detect sarcasm, mixed sentiment, or context-dependent irony, which are common in social media writing. Fourth, the LDA topic model was sensitive to the broad shared vocabulary in the corpus, which may explain why yearly topic averages remained so similar across years.

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
5.3 Future Directions
Future research could address these limitations in several ways. Structural topic modeling (STM), which explicitly models topic prevalence as a function of metadata variables such as year or engagement level, could yield more sensitive estimates of thematic change and its relationship to engagement. Using larger yearly strata or the full corpus would provide greater statistical power to detect subtle shifts in AI discourse. Integrating sentence-level or contextual sentiment models, such as transformer-based classifiers trained on social media data, could capture the nuanced and ironic tone of tweets more validly than word-level dictionaries. Finally, future work could examine specific AI milestones (such as the release of GPT-3 in 2020) as natural experiments to test whether particular events produced detectable shifts in the themes or sentiment of AI discourse on Twitter.

title: Evolution of Online AI Discourse
text:
5.4 Conclusion
This study finds that AI discourse on Twitter became steadily more positive and increasingly engaging between 2017 and 2021, without a fundamental reorganization of its underlying thematic structure. The evidence suggests that public AI discourse evolved through two primary mechanisms: increasing technical specialization, as users began labeling their tweets with more precise applied subfield hashtags, and growing optimism, as AI became more integrated into business and everyday technology conversation. Engagement with AI content was driven more by the growing public interest in AI over time than by the specific emotional tone or thematic focus of individual tweets. These findings contribute to a broader understanding of how transformative technologies are discursively normalized and adopted within public social media spaces.

# only contain valid nodes.

df0 = df.dropna(
    subset=[
        'tourist_visa',
        'source',
        'source_region',
        'source_subregion',
    ]
)

#【.dropna()】
# This method drops rows with any missing values (Pandas, 2024b).

#【subset】
# This parameter specifies which columns to consider when removing rows with 
# missing values. In this case, these are the columns to be used for 
# creating sankey diagram.

# For convenience, this project will only use data about tourist visa, 
# as the most common type of visa (Recchi et al., 2021, p. 7).




Defining Nodes


Code
##############################################################################
## Defining lists to store labels for nodes
source_region_labels = list(df0['source_region'].unique())
source_subregion_labels = list(df0['source_subregion'].unique())

#【.unique()】
# This method returns an Array object containing a list of unique values 
# from a Series object (Pandas, 2024f). In this case, the columns 
# 'source_region' and 'source_subregion' in the DataFrame df0 serve as 
# the Series objects, where missing values are already dropped via 
# .dropna() method. Subsequently, .unique() method is used to return 
# Array objects containing lists of unique values from each column. 
# These values will be used to create node labels. 

#【list()】
# This built-in Python function (also class) converts the input to 
# a list (Python, 2024a). In this case, it is used to convert the 
# Array objects mentioned above into Python list objects.

##############################################################################
## Combining lists to define an entire list storing all node labels
node_labels = ['World'] + source_region_labels + source_subregion_labels

#【+】
# This operator concatenates two or more sequences (e.g., lists) 
# into a single sequence (Python, 2024b). In this case, it is used 
# to combine lists of node labels into a single list.

#【'World'】
# 'World' is added to the start of the list, serving as the root node label.

##############################################################################
## Defining an an iterable range object to store the index for each node
node_ids = range(len(node_labels))

#【len()】
# This built-in Python function returns the number of items in an object 
# (i.e., the length of an object), which can be a sequence (e.g., list) 
# or a collection (e.g., dictionary) (Python, 2024a). In this case, 
# it is used to return the length of the list node_labels storing 
# all node labels, which equals to 23.

#【range()】
# This built-in Python function (also class) returns a sequence of numbers, 
# starting from zero by default (Python, 2024a). In this case, 
# range(23) starts from 0 to 22, which equal to the indices of items 
# in the list node_labels.

##############################################################################
## Defining a dictionary to store the label and index for each node
nodes = dict(zip(node_labels, node_ids))

#【zip()】
# This built-in Python function iterate over several iterables in parallel, 
# producing tuples with an item from each one (Python, 2024a).

#【dict()】
# This built-in Python function (also class) creates a dictionary from 
# two or more sequences (Python, 2024a). In this case, it is used 
# to create a dictionary where the keys are node labels 
# and values are the indices of node labels.




Defining Links


Code
##############################################################################
## Creating an aggregated DataFrame to store relevant data, which will be used 
# to calculate the value of link towards each subregion.
to_subregion_value = df0.groupby('source_subregion').agg(
    {
    'source_region':'first', # the region of each subregion 
    'tourist_visa':'mean', # mean of tourist visa cost in each subregion
    }
)

#【.groupby()】
# This method is used to group rows of df0 based on the column 'source_region'.

#【.agg()】
# This method is used to calculate the aggregation of values in each group.

#【'first'】
# This aggregation function returns the first entry of each column within 
# each group in the DataFrameGroupBy object (Pandas, 2024a). 
# In this case, it is used to select the first region encountered 
# in each group of subregions (i.e., the region for each subregion).

##############################################################################
## Creating an aggregated DataFrame to store values of link towards each region.
to_region_value = (
    to_subregion_value
    .groupby('source_region')
    .agg({'tourist_visa':'sum'})
)

##############################################################################
## Creating empty lists to store the source, source, and value for each link
sources = [] # to be filled with the indices of node labels of source nodes
targets = [] # to be filled with the indices of node labels of target nodes
values = [] # to be filled with the values for each link (tourist_visa)

## From 'World' to regions
for region in source_region_labels:

    # Append 'World' as the source nodes
    sources.append(nodes['World'])

    # Append each region as the target nodes
    targets.append(nodes[region])

    # Append values in df0_agg1 for each region as the value for each link
    values.append(to_region_value.loc[region,'tourist_visa'])

## From regions to subregions
for subregion in source_subregion_labels:

    # Append each corresponding region as the source nodes
    # get the corresponding region
    region = to_subregion_value.loc[subregion,'source_region']
    sources.append(nodes[region])

    # Append each subregion as the target nodes
    targets.append(nodes[subregion]) 
    
    # Append values in df0_agg2 for each subregion as the value for each link
    values.append(to_subregion_value.loc[subregion,'tourist_visa'])




Assigning Colors


Code
print("Source Regions:")
print(source_region_labels) # Checking regions in df0


Source Regions:
['Asia', 'Africa', 'Europe', 'Americas', 'Oceania']




Code
##############################################################################
## Defining a dictionary to store region colors

region_colors = {
    'Africa': '#6B3A09',  # Brown
    'Asia': '#7FA709',  # Green
    'Americas': '#EC6C92', # Pink
    'Oceania': '#F5DA29',  # Yellow
    'Europe': '#85D6D5',  # Blue
}

# These colors are generated (in hex) via Nichols's (no date) 
# color palette generator for better visibility to color blind people.

##############################################################################
## Defining node colors based on regions

node_colors = []  # Creating an empty list to store node colors

# Using for loop to append node colors to the list
for label in node_labels:
    if label in source_region_labels:
        node_colors.append(region_colors[label])
    
    elif label in source_subregion_labels:
        # get the corresponding region
        region = to_subregion_value.loc[label,'source_region']
        node_colors.append(region_colors[region])

    else: # if label is 'World'  
        node_colors.append('silver') 

##############################################################################
## Define link colors with transparency
import matplotlib.colors as c  # to create faint colors in RGBA format

## Defining strings to store faint colors for links in RGBA format
faint_Africa = 'rgba'+str(c.to_rgba(region_colors['Africa'], 0.5)) 
faint_Asia = 'rgba'+str(c.to_rgba(region_colors['Asia'], 0.5)) 
faint_Americas = 'rgba'+str(c.to_rgba(region_colors['Americas'], 0.5)) 
faint_Oceania = 'rgba'+str(c.to_rgba(region_colors['Oceania'], 0.5)) 
faint_Europe = 'rgba'+str(c.to_rgba(region_colors['Europe'], 0.5))

# 0.5: alpha=0.5, means 50% transparency of colors

##############################################################################
## Defining a dictionary to store faint link colors based on regions
faint_region_colors = {
    'Africa': faint_Africa,  # faint brown
    'Asia': faint_Asia,  # faint green
    'Americas': faint_Americas,  # faint pink
    'Oceania': faint_Oceania,  # faint yellow
    'Europe': faint_Europe,  # faint blue
}

##############################################################################
## Defining link colors based on regions
link_colors = []  # Creating an empty list to store link colors

# Using for loop to append link colors to the list
for source, target in zip(sources, targets):
    if node_labels[source] in source_region_labels:
        link_colors.append(faint_region_colors[node_labels[source]])
    
    elif node_labels[target] in source_region_labels:
        link_colors.append(faint_region_colors[node_labels[target]])

##############################################################################
# Check if the number of node colors matches the number of nodes
print('Number of node colors matches nodes?')
print(len(node_colors) == len(nodes))

# Check if the number of link colors matches the number of links
print('Number of link colors matches links?')
print(len(link_colors) == len(sources) == len(targets) == len(values))


Number of node colors matches nodes?
True
Number of link colors matches links?
True




Creating Sankey Diagram


Code
##############################################################################
import plotly.graph_objects as go  # to create sankey diagram

## Creating a Sankey object to store sankey data via go.Sankey() function

sankey_data = go.Sankey(   # defining a variable to store sankey data
    # defining nodes
    node = dict(
        label = node_labels,  # labels for nodes
        color = node_colors,  # colors for nodes
        pad = 30,  # the horizontal distance between nodes
        thickness = 20,  # thickness of nodes
        line = {'color':'black','width':0.5},  # style for node outlines
    ),

    # defining links
    link = dict(
        source = sources,  # indices of source nodes in node_labels
        target = targets,  # indices of target nodes in node_labels
        value = values,  # values for links
        color = link_colors, # colors for links
    )
)

##############################################################################
# Creating a Figure object to store sankey data via go.Figure() function
fig1 = go.Figure(sankey_data)

# Updating layout of the figure
fig1.update_layout(
    # width = 800, height = 600,
    margin = dict(t = 60, b = 10),  # margin top & bottom
    title_text = '''
        Sankey diagram of average tourist visa costs from each area
    ''',
    title_x = 0.5, title_y = 0.95,  # location of the title
    font = {'style':'italic'},      # font style of the figure
) 
    #【.update_layout()】
    # This method is used to update the layout of the figure, 
    # including the size, title, font, etc. (Plotly, 2024b).

# fig1.show(config = {"responsive": True})      # display the sankey diagram
fig1.show()      # display the sankey diagram

    #【renderer】
    # This parameter is used to specify the renderer used to render
    # the figure (Plotly, 2024b), and 'notebook' refers to Jupyter notebook.

    # This project is edited and exported to HTML file using
    # Visual Studio Code with Jupyter extensions, in which case
    # renderer='notebook' is effective for diagram display within
    # Visual Studio Code and the exported HTML file. However,
    # this argument seems not working for diagram display within output
    # cells using Jupyter (but working for exported HTML file).




                                                


Figure 1: Sankey diagram of average tourist visa costs from each area




As shown above, Figure 1 illustrates the distribution of average tourist visa costs from global regions (e.g., Asia, Europe, Africa) to their corresponding subregions (e.g., Eastern Asia, Northern Europe). The width of the links from regions to subregions represents the average tourist visa cost per country (as countries of origin) in each subregion, while the widths of the links on the left-hand side (from the ‘World’ node to each region node) are the sum of their corresponding links towards the subregions. Overall, this Sankey diagram (Figure 1) shows the flow of global tourist visa costs.






Figure 2: Sankey diagram of average tourist visa costs from each area (sorted)



Moreover, Figure 2 provides the same Sankey diagram with manually sorted subregion nodes by their links’ values (average tourist visa costs), which could be of better interpretation for gaps between areas in tourist visa costs. As shown in Figure 2, subregions belonging to Asia and Africa exhibit significantly larger flows, indicating their higher overall tourist visa cost. In contrast, smaller flows can be seen in Europe, Australia and New Zealand, and Northern America, reflecting their lower average tourist visa costs. Meanwhile, within Asia, the Southern Asia area has the highest figure whereas Eastern Asia has the lowest. Likewise, Western Europe has the lowest figure among all European areas.
In general, Figure 1 and Figure 2 reveal potential inequality between countries in different areas regarding the ability to leave their country. This gap will be further demonstrated in the following sections on the bar chart (Figure 3 and Figure 4).

title: Global Visa Cost Geographical Analysis
text:
2.2 Bar Chart

Aggregating Data


Code
import plotly.express as px  # to create plotly interactive graphs

# Aggregating data in order to draw the bar chart
bar_agg = df.groupby('source_subregion').agg(
    {
    'source_region':'first', # region of each subregion
    'tourist_visa':'mean',   # average tourist visa cost
    'tourist_perdailyincome':'mean', # average tourist visa cost per daily income
    }
).reset_index()  # resetting index to make 'source_subregion' a regular column

#【.reset_index()】
# This method is used to reset the index of the aggregated DataFrame, 
# placing all columns into a single level index (Pandas, 2024c). 
# Namely, transform the automatically generated index ('source_subregion') 
# into a regular column.




Creating Bar Chart


Code
# Creating a bar chart to show average tourist visa cost in each subregion

fig2 = px.bar(
    bar_agg, x = 'tourist_visa', y = 'source_subregion',
    labels = {  # assigning x and y labels 
        'tourist_visa':'Average tourist visa cost ($)',  
        'source_subregion':'Subregion',
    },
    color = 'source_region',  # grouping bars by region in different colors
    color_discrete_map = region_colors  # assigning colors (previously defined)
)

# Updating layout properties of the figure
fig2.update_layout(
    # width=800, height=480,
    margin = dict(t = 90, b = 10),    # margin top & bottom
    title_text='Average tourist visa costs per subregion',
    title_x = 0.5, title_y = 0.95,    # location of the title
    titlefont = {'style': 'italic'},  # font style of the title
    yaxis = {'categoryorder': 'total ascending'}, # sort by values ascending
    legend = {
        'title':'Region',
        'orientation': 'h',  # horizontal oriented legend
        'xanchor': 'right',
        'yanchor': 'bottom', 
        'x': 1, 'y': 1,
    },
) 

# fig2.show(config = {"responsive": True}) # displaying the figure
fig2.show() # displaying the figure




                                                


Figure 3: Bar chart of average tourist visa costs from each subregion




Figure 3 presents the same data as in Figure 1 and Figure 2, which is the average tourist visa costs from each area (only subregions in Figure 3), grouped by region in different colors, while the bar chart (Figure 3) can show the difference in value more clearly as compared to Figure 2 since the length in Figure 3 (bar chart) are more distinguishable than the width of links in Figure 1, which is also in line with Wilke’s (Wilke, 2019, Chapter 6) suggestion that bar chart could be more suitable for presenting variations in amount of value. It may be inferred from Figure 1, Figure 2 and Figure 3, that people in countries in well-developed areas (e.g., Western Europe) have to pay less to go abroad whereas those in underdeveloped countries in areas such as Southern Asia and Northern Africa should pay more to go abroad. Nevertheless, another variable ‘tourist_perdailyincome’ (see Appendix for detailed description) is introduced to calculate the underlying actual data on the level of tourist visa costs when adjusted by the average daily income in each country (Recchi et al., 2021, p. 4).


Code
# Creating a bar chart to show average tourist visa cost per daily income
# in each subregion, namely, the average working days to afford a
# tourist visa from countries in each subregion.

fig3 = px.bar(
    bar_agg, x = 'tourist_perdailyincome', y = 'source_subregion',
    labels = dict( # assigning x and y labels
        tourist_perdailyincome = '''
            Average working days to afford tourist visa (day)
        ''', 
        source_subregion = 'Subregion',
    ),
    color = 'source_region', # grouping by region in different colors
    color_discrete_map = region_colors # assigning colors (previously defined)
)

# Updating layout properties of the figure
fig3.update_layout(
    # width=800, height=480,
    margin = dict(t = 90, b = 10),    # margin top & bottom
    title_text='''
        Average working days to afford tourist visa per subregion
    ''',
    title_x = 0.5, title_y = 0.95,    # location of the title
    titlefont = {'style': 'italic'},  # font style of the title
    yaxis = {'categoryorder': 'total ascending'}, # sort by values ascending
    legend = {
        'title':'Region',
        'orientation': 'h',  # horizontal oriented legend
        'xanchor': 'right',
        'yanchor': 'bottom', 
        'x': 1, 'y': 1,
    },
) 

# fig3.show(config = {"responsive": True}) # displaying the figure
fig3.show() # displaying the figure




                                                


Figure 4: Average working days to afford tourist visa per subregion




As shown in Figure 4, there are even more significant gaps between areas regarding their income-adjusted average tourist visa costs (i.e., average working days to afford a tourist visa), revealing a paradoxical global situation – the poorer a country, the more citizens in it should pay for tourist visas to go abroad.

title: Global Visa Cost Geographical Analysis
text:
2.3 Choropleth

Aggregating Data


Code
# Aggregating data in order to draw the choropleth
choro_agg = (
    df.groupby('source')
    .agg({
        'source_iso3': 'first',
        'tourist_visa': 'mean',
    })
    .sort_values('tourist_visa', ascending=False)
    .reset_index()
)

#【.sort_values()】
# This method is used to sort the aggregated DataFrame by 'tourist_visa' 
# column, in descending order (ascending=False) (Pandas, 2024d).

#【.reset_index()】
# resetting index to make 'source_iso3' a regular column




Creating Choropleth


Code
# Creating a choropleth map to show average tourist visa cost in each country
fig4 = px.choropleth(
    choro_agg, 
    locations = 'source_iso3', # region identifiers for locations
    locationmode = 'ISO-3', # country codes
    color = 'tourist_visa', # map color by tourist visa costs
    hover_name = 'source', # hover: region name
    hover_data = 'tourist_visa', # hover: data
    color_continuous_scale = 'aggrnyl', # color scale for the choropleth
)

# Updating layout properties of the figure
fig4.update_layout(
    # width = 800, height = 500,
    title = 'Choropleth map of average tourist visa costs',
    margin = dict(t = 50, b = 0),
    title_x = 0.5, title_y = 0.95,  # location of the title
    title_font = {'style':'italic'},  # font style of the title
    coloraxis = dict(
        colorbar = dict(  # specify colorbar properties
            orientation = 'h',  # orientation of the colorbar: horizontal
            title = 'Average tourist visa cost ($)', # title of the colorbar
            xanchor = 'right', x = 1,
            yanchor = 'top', y = 1,
            len = 0.8, thickness = 16,  # length and thickness of the colorbar
        ),
    ),
)

# fig4.show(config = {"responsive": True}) # displaying the figure
fig4.show() # displaying the figure




                                                


Figure 5: Choropleth map of average tourist visa costs




Furthermore, we may gain insights by moving from the sub-region level to the country level. Figure 5 provides information about the distribution of average tourist visa costs in specific countries around the world, which varies with the continuous color indicator. According to Wilke (2019, p. 173), as for choropleth, it is typically accepted that dark-colored background areas in the maps behave better in visibility than light-colored background areas for webpages. Figure 5 thus employs a color map with dark-colored background areas, where the color gradually fades with the value increasing (Plotly, 2024).

title: Global Visa Cost Geographical Analysis
text:
Global Visa Cost Dataset
The Global Visa Cost Dataset, created by researchers from the European University Institute’s Migration Policy Centre, provides comprehensive data on visa costs for seven categories between country pairs in 2019. The dataset was developed through manual data collection primarily from official government websites and consulates.
Visa costs are coded in the following categories: tourist, student, business, work, family reunification, transit, and other motives, recorded in US dollars.
For countries where information was not available in English, French, Spanish, Arabic, Italian or Russian, a translation website was used to obtain the necessary information from the primary language of the country.
Several countries, including Palestine and the Syrian Arab Republic, are not coded due to a lack of information about travel access to their territory. Some countries needed a second round of visa cost checks due to difficulties in finding the information about their visa policy and visa requirements. In the case of many country pairs, there is visa policy information but there is no indication of the actual visa costs. For instance, Nauru in Oceania required a particularly engaging search to retrieve an official document on Immigration Regulations of January 2014, where the actual visa categories and costs are mentioned. This information is not indicated in any other official online source. For country pairs where information is not available in the dataset, missing values are introduced.

title: Global Visa Cost Geographical Analysis
text:
Variable Description

Variables: Countries and Regions
These variables represent the countries and regions of origin and destination, where ‘region’ refers to the continent a country is located in, and ‘subregion’ refers to sub-regions within continents.

source: Country of origin
target: Country of destination
source_iso3: ISO3 code of country of origin
target_iso3: ISO3 code of country of destination


source_region: World region country of origin is located in
target_region: World region country of destination is located in
source_subregion: World sub-region country of destination is located in
target_subregion: World sub-region country of destination is located in

Note: Regions and sub-regions are based on the UN M.49 GeoScheme.


Variables: Visa Costs to the Target Countries
These variables are visa costs to the target countries from the source countries based on the official visa policies and visa requirements of the target countries in 2019, measured in US dollars.

tourist_visa: Tourist visa costs (in USD)
student_visa: Student visa costs (in USD)
business_visa: Business visa costs (in USD)
work_visa: Work visa costs (in USD)
family_reunification_visa: Family reunification visa costs (in USD)
transit_visa: Transit visa costs (in USD)
other_motives_visa: Other motives visa costs (in USD)



Variables: Average Working Days to Pay Visa Costs
These variables estimates how many days people in the source countries have to work on average to be able to afford a visa to the target countries, measured in days.

tourist_perdailyincome: Tourist visa costs (in average daily incomes)
student_perdailyincome: Student visa costs (in average daily incomes)
business_perdailyincome: Business visa costs (in average daily incomes)
work_perdailyincome: Work visa costs (in average daily incomes)
family_perdailyincome: Family reunification costs (in average daily incomes)
transit_perdailyincome: Transit visa costs (in average daily incomes)
other_perdailyincome: Other motives visa costs (in average daily incomes)

Note: The authors used the GNI per capita as an approximation of the mean income of the average person in a country to calculate what the real costs of visas may be like. Information about GNI per capita are from World Bank (2019), which is available at: https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GNP.PCAP.CD .


Variable: Updates Compared to the Previous Version
This variable indicates updates to the previous version of the dataset.

updated: 1 if value was added/altered in the November 2020 update of the dataset from version 1.0 to version 1.1

Note: Updates in version 1.1 compared to version 1.0 include corrections, additional data for numerous countries, and a new variable ‘updated’ to track changes. This project only used the updated version (version 1.1) of the dataset.

title: Sankey Diagrams and Network Graphs
text:
7 min read · 1286 EN words · 0 中文字
Environment and Versions
python==3.12
pandas==2.2.2
plotly==5.24.1
matplotlib==3.9.x
pyvis==0.3.2
